rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Ritual / practice (as noun) / ceremony — hard ban

rules_evaluated.evidence: line 9: Godin-warm + Sutherland-reframe voice appropriate for Bootstrap trio

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Don't smuggle terms of art from maintainer into body
Value-prop / positioning framing belongs in sales copy + strategy, NOT in student-facing curriculum body
rules_evaluated.evidence: line 9 'A yardstick you rewrite is not a yardstick' is pedagogical, not positioning defense

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Watch out for planting jargon too early
rules_evaluated.evidence: line 11: 'generation and judging happen in separate subagents' introduces term without one-breath primer. Same at line 39. Subagent is a Claude Code primitive not yet earned in module body.
rules_evaluated.fix: Either (a) add one-breath primer on first use at line 11 — 'subagents are isolated Claude sessions that let multiple processes run in parallel' — then use the real term, or (b) use plain language 'separate sessions' at first use (lines 11, 39) and earn 'subagent' in a later module where it lands operationally in the exercise body.

rules_evaluated.evidence: Exercise frames the student as becoming someone who sets up eval infrastructure, not as fixing a deficit

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Earn every technical term on first use
rules_evaluated.evidence: line 11: 'subagents' appears in body prose without a one-breath primer tied to what the student just did or is about to do
rules_evaluated.fix: Provide a single-sentence earn before line 11: 'Subagents are isolated execution contexts within Claude Code — each one gets fresh context for a specific task.' Then introduce the term.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Always 'you', never 'the student'; teacher framing stays out of body
rules_evaluated.evidence: Lines 4–39 consistently use second person 'you' for student actions; no teacher-behavior narration in body

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Section headers use command verbs when the section is about doing something
rules_evaluated.evidence: 'Phase 0: Run the judge once', 'Phase 1: Start the loop', 'Phase 2: Come back to the notes' — all verb-lead

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Every prompt block gets a one-sentence action lead-in
rules_evaluated.evidence: line 19 provides action lead-in for Phase 0 prompt; Phase 1 and 2 prompts similarly preceded

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Canonical presentation shape — lead-in, label, blank line, fenced block
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 0 (lines 19–31): lead-in at 19, blank 20, **Prompt** 21, fence 23–31. Correct.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Runtime-fork prompt blocks: paired divs, shared chip
rules_evaluated.evidence: lines 41–122: .rt-code and .rt-cowork divs both use *(Claude Code)* label; renderer auto-swaps per runtime

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Artifact destinations are explicit subpaths, never naked filenames (Bootstrap-specific)
rules_evaluated.evidence: lines 26, 28, 49, etc. all use 'module-6/...' subpaths; no naked filenames

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Subagent use — explain why after the prompt
rules_evaluated.evidence: lines 41–81 (.rt-code) and 82–122 (.rt-cowork) prompt blocks use 'subagent'/'agent' but no explanation follows (at line 124+) of why the separation matters pedagogically or mechanically
rules_evaluated.fix: After line 122 (before line 124), add one sentence: 'Subagents keep the generator and judge isolated — each gets fresh context and neither can see the other working, so the judge can't be biased by the generator's intermediate thinking.' This explains the design without adding noise to the prompt itself.

rules_evaluated.evidence: Exercise does not have Big Idea / Key Concepts sections; mood tie-in checks do not apply to exercise files
Exercise is not buyer-facing copy; sales-copy rules do not apply
